Sample Digest · Monday 27 April 2026

A recent Oncology digest,
as sent to subscribers

This is the real Monday digest from Monday 27 April 2026. Showing the first 8 of 13 articles from that week — every new paper from the top journals in Oncology.

13articles that week
2journals covered
£0for your first 4 weeks
Note: Summaries are AI-generated from publicly available PubMed abstracts. Always verify against the source article before clinical use.
Annals of oncology : official journal of the European Society for Medical Oncology

Including tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes into the PREDICT prognostic model for triple-negative breast cancer survival

Wang Y, Drubay D, Jonas SF et al. · 2026 Apr 21
Study Type: Retrospective cohort study with model development and internal-external validation
Key Question: Can incorporating stromal tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (sTILs) into the PREDICT prognostic model improve chemotherapy decision-making for women with early-stage triple-negative breast cancer?
Key Findings:
  • PREDICT_sTILs demonstrated good calibration (O/E ratios 0.98-0.99) and discrimination (AUC 0.74) at 5 and 10 years in 3698 TNBC patients
  • Compared to standard PREDICT, the enhanced model identified 19-60 additional net true low-risk patients per 1000 chemotherapy-naïve patients at 10-year mortality risk thresholds of 8-15%
  • Model particularly improved identification of low-risk patients who could safely avoid chemotherapy
Clinical Relevance: This could help UK oncologists make more personalised chemotherapy decisions in early-stage TNBC, potentially sparing some patients unnecessary treatment toxicity while maintaining prognostic accuracy.
Limitations: Retrospective design with patients diagnosed over a 38-year period may not reflect contemporary treatment practices and outcomes.
PubMed →DOI →
Annals of oncology : official journal of the European Society for Medical Oncology

Colorectal cancer detection using non-contrast CT and deep learning: a multicenter and international cohort study

Chen X, Qiu MY, Zhang JP et al. · 2026 Apr 21
Study Type: Retrospective multicenter international cohort study with AI development and validation
Key Question: Can a deep learning algorithm (COCA) using non-contrast CT scans effectively detect colorectal cancer for opportunistic screening?
Key Findings:
  • COCA achieved AUC 0.967-0.996 across six validation centers (n=2,053), improving radiologist sensitivity by 20.4% and specificity by 5.4%
  • Real-world validation in 27,433 consecutive patients showed sensitivity 86.6-88.2% and specificity 99.5-99.8%
  • Positive predictive value was 63.4% in the larger real-world cohort (n=18,427)
Clinical Relevance: This could enable opportunistic colorectal cancer screening using routine CT scans already performed in NHS hospitals, potentially identifying cancers without additional procedures or patient burden.
Limitations: Retrospective design with potential selection bias, and the 63.4% positive predictive value suggests significant false positive rates that could impact clinical workflows.
PubMed →DOI →
Annals of oncology : official journal of the European Society for Medical Oncology

Re-Evaluating Antibody-Drug Conjugate Linker Stability: Assessment, Interpretation, and Clinical Translation

Colombo R, Seredick S, Barnscher SD et al. · 2026 Apr 21
Study Type: Commentary/review article
Key Question: How should linker stability be evaluated and optimized in antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) development for cancer treatment?
Key Findings:
  • Several clinically successful ADCs use relatively unstable linkers, challenging the assumption that stable linkers are superior
  • ADCs with increased linker stability have not consistently shown improved clinical outcomes and may cause unexpected toxicities
  • Preclinical models have significant limitations in predicting human payload exposure patterns
Clinical Relevance: This challenges current ADC development paradigms and could inform more rational design strategies for next-generation ADCs entering UK clinical trials and practice.
Limitations: As a commentary rather than original research, conclusions depend on interpretation of existing data rather than new evidence generation.
PubMed →DOI →
Annals of oncology : official journal of the European Society for Medical Oncology

IO102-IO103 immune-modulatory cancer vaccine and pembrolizumab in melanoma

Hassel JC, Arance A, Carlino MS et al. · 2026 Apr 21
Study Type: Phase 3 randomized controlled trial
Key Question: Does adding IO102-IO103 immune-modulatory cancer vaccine to pembrolizumab improve progression-free survival in untreated advanced melanoma?
Key Findings:
  • Median PFS was 19.4 months with combination versus 11.0 months with pembrolizumab alone (HR 0.77, 95% CI 0.58-1.00, P=0.0558) - missed statistical significance threshold
  • Greater benefit observed in PD-L1-negative tumours (16.6 vs 3.0 months, HR 0.54) and anti-PD-1 naïve patients (24.8 vs 11.0 months, HR 0.74)
  • Similar grade ≥3 adverse event rates (14.5% vs 15.6%) with mostly mild injection site reactions (56% of patients)
Clinical Relevance: This combination approach could potentially benefit selected melanoma patients, particularly those with PD-L1-negative disease who typically respond poorly to pembrolizumab monotherapy.
Limitations: The study failed to meet its primary endpoint for statistical significance despite showing a numerical improvement in progression-free survival.
PubMed →DOI →
Journal of clinical oncology : official journal of the American Society of Clinical Oncology

First-in-Human, Phase I Study of Sigvotatug Vedotin, an Integrin Beta-6-Directed Antibody-Drug Conjugate: Results From Dose Expansion in Advanced Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer

Peters S, Piha-Paul SA, Sehgal K et al. · 2026 Apr 20
Study Type: Phase I dose-expansion study
Key Question: What is the safety, tolerability, and antitumor activity of sigvotatug vedotin, an integrin beta-6-directed antibody-drug conjugate, in patients with advanced non-small cell lung cancer?
Key Findings:
  • Overall objective response rate was 19% with median duration of response 11.3 months; in nonsquamous, taxane-naïve patients, response rate was 29% with 12.8-month median duration
  • Grade ≥3 treatment-emergent adverse events occurred in 48% of all patients (35% with optimal 1.8 mg/kg adjusted ideal body weight dosing)
  • Pharmacokinetic modelling supported 1.8 mg/kg adjusted ideal body weight every 2 weeks as the optimal regimen with lower variability
Clinical Relevance: This novel antibody-drug conjugate shows promising activity in heavily pre-treated advanced NSCLC patients, potentially offering a new treatment option for patients who have exhausted standard therapies including chemotherapy and immunotherapy.
Limitations: Single-arm phase I study design limits interpretation of efficacy compared to standard care.
PubMed →DOI →
Journal of clinical oncology : official journal of the American Society of Clinical Oncology

Durvalumab in Combination With Neoadjuvant Chemotherapy in Early Triple-Negative Breast Cancer: Long-Term Analysis From the GeparNuevo Trial

Loibl S, Untch M, Huober J et al. · 2026 Apr 20
Study Type: Phase II randomized controlled trial with long-term follow-up analysis
Key Question: Does adding durvalumab to neoadjuvant chemotherapy improve long-term survival outcomes in early triple-negative breast cancer patients?
Key Findings:
  • At 86.4 months median follow-up, durvalumab significantly improved invasive disease-free survival (HR 0.56, 95% CI 0.32-0.99, p=0.043), distant disease-free survival (HR 0.41, 95% CI 0.21-0.80), and overall survival (HR 0.33, 95% CI 0.14-0.79)
  • Greatest benefit observed in node-positive patients (iDFS HR 0.33, 95% CI 0.144-0.771)
  • Patients with high stromal TILs (>10%) in residual disease had 7-year iDFS of 92.3% versus 51.4% with low TILs
Clinical Relevance: This challenges current UK practice of continuing immunotherapy adjuvantly, suggesting neoadjuvant-only durvalumab may provide sustained survival benefit in early TNBC without requiring prolonged treatment.
Limitations: Small sample size (174 patients) limits power for subgroup analyses and generalizability of findings.
PubMed →DOI →
Journal of clinical oncology : official journal of the American Society of Clinical Oncology

Long-Term Efficacy and Safety of Taletrectinib in Patients With ROS1+ Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer: Results From the Phase II TRUST-I Study

Li W, Zhang Y, Fan H et al. · 2026 Apr 21
Study Type: Phase II single-arm study
Key Question: What is the long-term efficacy and safety of taletrectinib in patients with ROS1-positive advanced non-small cell lung cancer?
Key Findings:
  • In 103 TKI-naïve patients: 90.3% objective response rate (95% CI 82.9-95.3%) with median duration of response and progression-free survival both exceeding 4 years (49.7 and 49.6 months respectively)
  • In 66 crizotinib-pretreated patients: 51.5% response rate with median progression-free survival of 7.6 months and overall survival of 25.6 months
  • Safety profile remained manageable with no new safety signals identified during extended follow-up
Clinical Relevance: These results support taletrectinib as a highly effective first-line option for ROS1-positive NSCLC patients, with particularly impressive durability that could influence treatment sequencing decisions in UK practice.
Limitations: Single-arm design limits comparative assessment against current standard-of-care ROS1 inhibitors.
PubMed →DOI →
Journal of clinical oncology : official journal of the American Society of Clinical Oncology

Amivantamab Monotherapy in Chemorefractory RAS/BRAF Wild-Type Metastatic Colorectal Cancer: Results From OrigAMI-1, an Open-Label, Phase Ib/II Study

Oberstein PE, Hecht JR, Raghav K et al. · 2026 Apr 21
Study Type: Phase Ib/II open-label single-arm study
Key Question: Does amivantamab monotherapy demonstrate efficacy in chemorefractory RAS/BRAF wild-type metastatic colorectal cancer patients with 2-3 prior treatment lines?
Key Findings:
  • Objective response rates were 29% (5/17) in left-sided anti-EGFR naive patients, 19% (10/54) in left-sided anti-EGFR exposed patients, and 22% (5/23) in right-sided patients
  • Median progression-free survival ranged from 3.7-5.7 months across cohorts, with duration of response 6.1-9.8 months
  • Grade ≥3 treatment-related adverse events were infrequent: rash (7%), acneiform dermatitis (4%), hypoalbuminemia (4%)
Clinical Relevance: This provides a potential new treatment option for heavily pre-treated RAS/BRAF wild-type mCRC patients, a population with limited therapeutic choices after standard chemotherapy and targeted therapy failure in UK practice.
Limitations: Single-arm design without comparator arm limits interpretation of efficacy relative to current standard of care or best supportive care.
PubMed →DOI →
